response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: I am not someone who works in science or technology, so I can only speak from what I have observed and experienced in my own limited way. From what I understand, stereotype threat in STEM means that people perform worse when they are reminded that their group is supposedly not good at these subjects. Women, for instance, are told they cannot do mathematics or engineering as well as men. This idea gets into their heads and makes them anxious, which then affects their actual performance. It becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy. I left school early, and my parents had even less education than me, so I know what it feels like when society expects nothing from you. That pressure, that assumption you will fail, it weighs on you constantly. Whether we should address stereotype threat is not really a question in my mind. Of course we should, but I am skeptical about whether anything will actually change. People talk about these issues, organizations make statements, but the fundamental structures remain the same. I have worked retail for years and seen how management treats different people based on their background or appearance. The same patterns exist everywhere. As for how to address it, the practical solutions seem straightforward but difficult to implement. Stop telling girls they are bad at math. Stop assuming certain people cannot succeed in technical fields. Provide actual support rather than empty encouragement. But here is my honest view: those in power benefit from these stereotypes, consciously or not. They use them to keep certain groups out of well-paying fields. Real change would require dismantling systems that many people profit from, and I do not see much appetite for that. I practice Buddhism and try to accept what I cannot control, but that does not mean I think the situation is acceptable. It just means I have learned not to expect much from institutions or from people who refuse to examine their own biases. Stereotype threat is real, it harms people, and we should fight it, but I remain doubtful about whether meaningful action will follow all the talk.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona has low education level, parents with minimal education, works retail part-time, and scores very high on neuroticism with depression. This suggests someone who may feel excluded from academic/professional discussions and has a pessimistic, anxious worldview. Low openness means resistance to abstract concepts and preference for concrete thinking. Very low agreeableness indicates skepticism, bluntness, and distrust of others' motives. High conscientiousness means she thinks carefully and expresses views systematically despite limited formal education. Buddhism suggests some acceptance of suffering but not passivity about injustice. The tone should be direct, somewhat cynical, grounded in personal experience rather than theory, skeptical of institutions and change, but still acknowledging the problem exists. She would relate stereotype threat to her own experiences of low expectations based on class/education background rather than technical STEM knowledge.
response_parsed.tone: skeptical, direct, somewhat pessimistic, grounded in personal experience, blunt, resigned but not entirely passive, working-class perspective
